subtopic_detail: Aortic dissection imaging and initial pharmacotherapy
stem: You are the ED registrar at a tertiary hospital seeing a 64-year-old man with sudden severe tearing interscapular pain. BP is 184/108 in the right arm and 142/82 in the left arm. He has a long history of hypertension and is on perindopril. He is now diaphoretic but alert. Pulses are present in all four limbs. ECG shows sinus rhythm with LVH but no ischaemic changes. He has no contrast allergy and creatinine is 92 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 184/108 right arm, 142/82 left arm, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, T 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, alert, palpable pulses all four limbs, no focal neurological deficit, no abdominal pain
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm LVH, CXR widened mediastinum, troponin negative, creatinine 92, D-dimer 3.8 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send for urgent transthoracic echocardiogram as the definitive first-line diagnostic test for aortic dissection
options.rationale: TTE has limited sensitivity for descending dissection; CT angiography is the standard first-line investigation with rapid availability. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Commence intravenous heparin infusion empirically until CT pulmonary angiogram has excluded clot
options.rationale: Heparin is contraindicated until dissection is excluded because it can cause catastrophic haemorrhage from the false lumen; image first. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Start intravenous nitroprusside infusion first without beta blocker to drop SBP under 110 mmHg fast
options.rationale: Vasodilator without prior rate control causes reflex tachycardia, increasing shear stress and dissection propagation; beta blocker is initiated first. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Arrange CT angiography of the aorta and start IV esmolol then sodium nitroprusside to control BP
options.rationale: CT angiogram aorta is the first-line investigation in suspected dissection; rate control with esmolol before vasodilator nitroprusside prevents reflex tachycardia and propagation. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Discharge home with oral perindopril increase to 8 mg daily once routine bloodwork is unremarkable
options.rationale: Differential blood pressure with sudden tearing pain and a widened mediastinum is dissection until proven otherwise; discharge would be catastrophic. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection presents with tearing chest or back pain, differential pulses or BP, and mediastinal widening. CT angiography of the chest, abdomen and pelvis is the standard imaging. Type A dissection involves the ascending aorta and requires urgent cardiothoracic surgery. Type B is medically managed unless complicated. Initial pharmacotherapy targets HR under 60 and SBP 100 to 120 mmHg using intravenous esmolol or labetalol first, then nitroprusside or glyceryl trinitrate for ongoing blood pressure control. Analgesia with morphine is administered alongside.
key_points: Image with CT angiography of the aorta urgently.
Beta blocker before vasodilator to prevent reflex tachycardia.
Type A dissection needs urgent cardiothoracic surgery.
Avoid anticoagulation until dissection is excluded.
explanation.pearls: Initial Type A dissection mortality is around 1 percent per hour for the first 48 hours; phone the on-call cardiothoracic surgeon before the CT report is finalised if the patient is haemodynamically unstable or pulse deficits are progressive.

subtopic_detail: Dabigatran reversal with idarucizumab in major bleeding
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 78-year-old woman with a confirmed traumatic intracerebral haemorrhage of 30 mL on CT after a fall. She takes dabigatran 150 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation. Her last dose was 4 hours ago. She has a GCS of 12 and is being prepared for neurosurgical input. She has no other medications and no chronic kidney disease. The hospital pharmacy stocks idarucizumab, prothrombin complex concentrate and tranexamic acid.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 174/96, HR 78, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, T 36.6 °C, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5)
data_table.Examination: Right hemiparesis MRC 3/5, slurred speech, no other focal deficit, pupils equal reactive
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L, platelets 220, creatinine 85, INR 1.3, dTT pending, CT brain 30 mL right thalamic haematoma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer prothrombin complex concentrate 25 IU/kg now and recheck the INR after 30 minutes
options.rationale: PCC is the agent of choice for warfarin or factor Xa inhibitor reversal; dabigatran requires the specific reversal antibody idarucizumab as first-line. (Source: NBA DOAC Reversal)

options.text: Administer intravenous idarucizumab 5 g as two 2.5 g boluses given 15 minutes apart now urgently
options.rationale: Idarucizumab is the specific monoclonal antibody fragment reversing dabigatran and is indicated for life-threatening bleeding in patients on dabigatran in Australia. (Source: NBA DOAC Reversal)

options.text: Give intravenous vitamin K 10 mg and fresh frozen plasma 15 mL/kg to correct her coagulopathy quickly
options.rationale: Vitamin K and FFP are for warfarin reversal; they do not reverse the direct thrombin inhibition by dabigatran in significant time. (Source: NBA DOAC Reversal)

options.text: Commence intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes as the sole haemostatic agent for ICH now
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid does not reverse dabigatran and is not standard therapy for spontaneous ICH; idarucizumab is required for dabigatran reversal. (Source: NBA DOAC Reversal)

options.text: Arrange urgent haemodialysis to remove dabigatran prior to commencing any specific reversal therapy
options.rationale: Dialysis removes dabigatran but takes hours; idarucizumab acts within minutes and is the first-line intervention in active major bleeding. (Source: NBA DOAC Reversal)

explanation.summary: Idarucizumab is a monoclonal antibody fragment that binds dabigatran with high affinity and reverses anticoagulant effect within minutes. Indications include life-threatening or uncontrolled major bleeding and emergency surgery in patients on dabigatran. Dose is 5 g intravenously as two 2.5 g boluses. Andexanet alfa is the equivalent for apixaban and rivaroxaban, with PCC 25 to 50 IU/kg as alternative when unavailable. Local pharmacy stock and approval pathways should be confirmed in advance for major centres.
key_points: Idarucizumab 5 g IV in two boluses reverses dabigatran fast.
Andexanet alfa or PCC reverses Xa inhibitors.
Vitamin K and FFP do not reverse DOACs.
Confirm pharmacy stock and pathway in advance.
explanation.pearls: About 10 percent of patients re-anticoagulate within 12 to 24 hours after idarucizumab if intrinsic clearance is delayed by renal impairment; recheck thrombin time at 12 and 24 hours and consider a second dose if bleeding recurs.

subtopic_detail: Giant cell arteritis biopsy and steroid timing
stem: You are the ED registrar seeing a 76-year-old woman with two weeks of new daily temporal headache, jaw claudication after a few bites, and a tender thickened right temporal artery. Vision is currently normal. She has had no transient visual loss and no diplopia. She has had no prior steroid use and has no diabetes. She has an enlarged thyroid but is biochemically euthyroid. Her ESR is 92 mm/h and CRP is 110 mg/L. The vascular team can perform temporal artery biopsy within 7 days.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82, HR 76, RR 16, T 36.7 °C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Tender thickened right temporal artery, no pulses; visual acuity 6/9 bilaterally, no relative afferent pupillary defect
data_table.Investigations: ESR 92 mm/h, CRP 110 mg/L, FBC normal, platelets 380, no anaemia, eye fundoscopy normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg today and arrange temporal artery biopsy within the next week
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends immediate high-dose corticosteroid in suspected GCA to prevent visual loss; biopsy within 2 weeks remains diagnostic despite early steroid. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Rheumatology)

options.text: Defer all steroid therapy until temporal artery biopsy histology has confirmed the underlying diagnosis
options.rationale: Delay in starting steroid risks permanent visual loss; biopsy remains positive for around 2 weeks after corticosteroid commencement. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Rheumatology)

options.text: Start intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days only for any patient with suspected GCA
options.rationale: Pulsed IV steroid is reserved for visual loss or visual symptoms; oral high-dose prednisolone is appropriate in uncomplicated suspected GCA. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Rheumatology)

options.text: Commence indomethacin 50 mg three times daily and review again with ESR result in two weeks time
options.rationale: NSAIDs do not prevent ischaemic complications of GCA and steroid therapy is mandatory; delay is dangerous. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Rheumatology)

options.text: Commence low-dose prednisolone 15 mg daily and arrange same-day temporal artery duplex ultrasound study
options.rationale: Low-dose steroid is insufficient for GCA; ultrasound is adjunctive and does not replace high-dose corticosteroid plus biopsy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Rheumatology)

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis is a medical emergency because of risk of permanent vision loss from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy. Immediate high-dose corticosteroid 1 mg/kg orally is recommended on clinical suspicion before biopsy. Visual symptoms warrant intravenous methylprednisolone 500 to 1000 mg daily for 3 days. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic for around two weeks after starting steroid. Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing for relapsing or refractory disease. Bone protection, gastric prophylaxis and infection screening accompany prolonged glucocorticoid therapy.
key_points: Start prednisolone 1 mg/kg orally on clinical suspicion.
Use IV methylprednisolone if visual symptoms are present.
Biopsy remains positive for about 2 weeks after starting steroid.
Tocilizumab is the steroid-sparing agent for relapsing disease.
explanation.pearls: Once one eye is affected by anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy in GCA, the contralateral eye becomes involved within 1 to 2 weeks in about 25 to 50 percent of untreated cases; immediate steroid plus ophthalmology review is mandatory.

subtopic_detail: Phaeochromocytoma crisis alpha then beta blockade
stem: You are the ED registrar managing a 44-year-old man with episodic severe headaches, palpitations and sweating. His BP today is 218/124 with HR 124. He has had no antihypertensive therapy. Recent imaging from an outside hospital showed a 5 cm adrenal mass on CT. Plasma free metanephrines from a prior workup were markedly elevated. He has had no prior cardiovascular events. He is now in the resuscitation cubicle with arterial line and ICU is aware. Endocrine surgery has been contacted.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 218/124, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 97% room air, T 36.8 °C, BSL 9.2
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, alert, no focal neurology, no chest pain currently, mild tremor
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia LVH; plasma normetanephrine markedly elevated; CT 5 cm right adrenal mass; troponin negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management?

options.text: Start intravenous metoprolol 5 mg now and add phenoxybenzamine after rate control has been achieved
options.rationale: Beta blockade before alpha blockade in phaeochromocytoma causes unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and hypertensive crisis; this sequence is contraindicated. (Source: ESE Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Give intravenous hydralazine 10 mg as a single bolus to lower the blood pressure rapidly first now
options.rationale: Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia in catecholamine excess and is not first line; alpha blockade is the priority. (Source: ESE Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Begin intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus to provide combined alpha and beta blockade in one rapid step
options.rationale: Labetalol has predominant beta blockade and weak alpha blockade and is not adequate alpha blockade for phaeochromocytoma crisis. (Source: ESE Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence intravenous phentolamine 5 mg boluses and titrated infusion, defer beta blocker until alpha block is established
options.rationale: Acute phaeochromocytoma crisis is managed with alpha blockade first using IV phentolamine; beta blockade is added once alpha block is established to prevent unopposed alpha effects. (Source: ESE Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g as a single bolus then continue infusion in lieu of any alpha blocker
options.rationale: Magnesium can be adjunctive in phaeochromocytoma crisis but does not replace alpha blockade which is the cornerstone of management. (Source: ESE Phaeochromocytoma)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma crisis is managed by establishing alpha adrenergic blockade first to prevent unopposed alpha vasoconstriction. Phentolamine intravenously is the agent of choice during a crisis, with phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin orally during preparation for surgery. Beta blockade with propranolol or metoprolol is added only after alpha blockade is established, to control reflex tachycardia. Adequate sodium loading and volume repletion are required because chronic catecholamine excess contracts the intravascular volume. Definitive treatment is surgical resection after 7 to 14 days of pre-operative blockade.
key_points: Always block alpha receptors before beta in phaeochromocytoma.
Phentolamine IV is the acute alpha blocker of choice.
Adequate sodium loading precedes surgery.
Definitive treatment is laparoscopic adrenalectomy.
explanation.pearls: Aim for postural blood pressure below 120/80 sitting and not below 90/45 standing on alpha blockade, with mild nasal congestion as a clinical marker of adequate blockade before surgery is planned by the multidisciplinary team.

subtopic_detail: Rhabdomyolysis fluid resuscitation and hyperkalaemia management
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 24-year-old man brought in after 18 hours immobile on the floor following a methamphetamine binge. He has dark urine, swollen tender thighs and is oliguric. His CK is 86,000 IU/L. He has had no prior renal disease. He is alert but distressed by limb pain. Trauma has been excluded by primary and secondary survey. He has no medications. The intensive care registrar has been contacted regarding admission.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82, HR 102, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, T 36.9 °C, UO 0.2 mL/kg/h
data_table.Examination: Tense tender swollen thighs bilaterally, sensation intact, distal pulses intact, no compartment syndrome features yet
data_table.Investigations: CK 86,000 IU/L, K 6.4 mmol/L, creatinine 280, urea 18, pH 7.28, bicarbonate 16, urinalysis blood 3+ no RBC, ECG peaked T waves V2-V4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous insulin 10 units with dextrose 25 g only and reassess potassium after 30 minutes today
options.rationale: Insulin and dextrose lower potassium but ECG changes also require immediate membrane stabilisation with calcium before serum potassium-lowering measures. (Source: KHA-CARI AKI)

options.text: Give intravenous frusemide 80 mg bolus to drive forced diuresis as the cornerstone of myoglobin clearance
options.rationale: Loop diuretics do not provide protective renal benefit in rhabdomyolysis; volume resuscitation is the priority and frusemide may worsen the situation if hypovolaemic. (Source: KHA-CARI AKI)

options.text: Commence haemodialysis empirically to remove myoglobin and lower the serum creatine kinase fast
options.rationale: Myoglobin is not effectively cleared by dialysis; dialysis is reserved for severe AKI with classical indications. (Source: KHA-CARI AKI)

options.text: Begin intravenous mannitol 1 g/kg as the recommended renal-protective osmotic diuretic in rhabdomyolysis
options.rationale: Mannitol does not improve renal outcomes in rhabdomyolysis and can worsen AKI if hypovolaemic; isotonic crystalloid is preferred. (Source: KHA-CARI AKI)

options.text: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution and start aggressive isotonic crystalloid fluid
options.rationale: ECG changes with K 6.4 require immediate calcium gluconate for membrane stabilisation, plus aggressive isotonic crystalloid to maintain urine output above 200 mL/h in rhabdomyolysis. (Source: KHA-CARI AKI)

explanation.summary: Rhabdomyolysis can cause AKI through myoglobin precipitation, ischaemia and oxidative injury, especially with hypovolaemia. Australian and KDIGO guidance recommends early aggressive isotonic crystalloid to keep urine output around 200 to 300 mL/h. Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is treated with calcium gluconate first for membrane stabilisation, then insulin dextrose, salbutamol and sodium bicarbonate to shift potassium intracellularly. Renal replacement therapy is indicated for refractory hyperkalaemia, severe acidosis, uraemic complications or volume overload. Compartment syndrome should be reassessed serially.
key_points: Calcium gluconate stabilises membranes immediately.
Aim for urine output 200 to 300 mL/h with isotonic fluid.
Avoid loop diuretics and mannitol routinely.
Dialysis is reserved for refractory complications.
explanation.pearls: Compartment syndrome can develop with high CK and tense tender muscles even after initial neurovascular exam is normal; serial compartment pressure measurement and early fasciotomy consultation with orthopaedics prevents irreversible muscle and nerve damage.
